coredata.dc:title: A colorimetric sensor array for protein discrimination based on carbon nanodots-induced reversible aggregation of AuNP with GSH as a regulator
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jingwei
Lu, Yuexiang
He, Liuying
Pang, Jiawei
Yang, Fengyi
Liu, Yueying
coredata.dc:description: We have proposed a doubled nanoplasmonic colorimetric sensor array for protein recognition based on car-bon nanodots (CDs)-induced reversible aggregation of gold nanoparticle (AuNP) with GSH as a regulator. It is found that different kinds of CDs can induce the aggregation of AuNP with different degrees and cause different color change. Adding reduced glutathione (GSH) into the CDs/AuNP system can make the AuNP disperse again accompanying with a second color change. Based on this phenomenon, three kinds of CDs originated from different amino acids and citric acid are used to fabricate a colorimetric sensor array with three sensing elements. Upon addition of target proteins into CDs/AuNP conjugates, the competitive protein-AuNP affinity disrupts the SPR absorption peak of CDs/AuNP, generating cross-reactive three-unit colorimetric signal. Subsequently, GSH is introduced into each unit containing protein samples in situ. The SPR absorption peak of AuNP is substantially affected due to the stronger affinity between AuNP and GSH, producing an-other three-unit response signal. With this strategy, each sensing element can give two output signals to each target protein, which doubles the number of unit from three to six without changing the three-cell configuration, resulting in greatly improved discrimination ability of proteins and identification accuracy of unknown samples. Twelve proteins at 50 nM can be well discriminated by this novel colorimetric sensor array. Moreover, our sensing system is very stable in the high concen-tration of salt, make it suitable for the real sample analysis. This method effectively identifies twelve proteins at 200 nM spiked in human urine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pattern discrimination of proteins
Doubled sensing elements
Colorimetric sensor array
CDs/AuNP conjugates
Reversible aggregation
GSH as a regulator

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reversible aggregation of AuNP induced by CDs and GSH
Stability of reversible aggregation of AuNP induced by CDs and GSH
Design of a doubled colorimetric sensing elements based on reversible aggregation of CDs/AuNP conjugates
A doubled colorimetric sensor array for discrimination of multiple proteins

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Six-unit signal from the doubled nanoplasmonic colorimetric sensor array with three-unit configuration for protein discrimination based on CDs-induced reversible aggregation AuNP with GSH as a regulator.
The inside photographs in Fig. 1B and Fig .1D reveal the aggregation state of AuNP, which is corresponding to the columns below.

The inserted photographs show the aggregation state of AuNP changing through time. In Fig. 2A, AuNP shows unstable aggregation state in the presence of NaCl, the photos reveal the lighten color within hours. On the contrary, in Fig. 2B-D, AuNP/CDs shows very stable aggregation state with or without NaCl, the color of AuNP doesn’t change within hours.

In Fig. 3A and B, the inserted photographs reveal the aggregation state of AuNP in different systems without or with GSH. For Fig. 3A, photos No.2 and No.3 indicate that AuNP aggregates in the presence of s-CDs and l-CDs, the solution color changes to purple and dark blue. After adding GSH, in Fig. 3B, AuNP exhibits disperse state and the solution color changes back to red, as can be seen in photos No.5 and No.6.

Particle size distribution of DLS of left column and corresponding TEM images right column for different systems. (A) AuNP; (B) AuNP + IgG + s-CDs; (C) AuNP + IgG + l-CDs; (D) AuNP + IgG + g-CDs; (E) AuNP + IgG; (F) AuNP + IgG + s-CDs + GSH; (G) AuNP + IgG + l-CDs + GSH; (H) AuNP + IgG + g-CDs + GSH.

Array-based sensing of twelve proteins. (A) The recognition patterns of response signal (k/k0) in the presence of proteins at 50 nM (responses are an average of six measurements and the error bars are the standard deviation); (B) Photograph of the color change upon addition of protein solutions at 50 nM.

Twelve proteins were clustered by LDA with the colorimetric responses from only three sensing elements. (A) The signals of three units without the addition of GSH; (B) The signals of three units with only the addition of GSH; (C) All signals of six units including before and after the addition of GSH; (D) Correct unknown identification percentage of three sensing systems. There are 24 unknown samples for twelve proteins (Tables S5–S7).

(A) Two dimensional LDA plot for 12 proteins discrimination spiked into urine samples against three sensing units with six readout signals; (B) Hierarchical cluster analysis dendrogram for proteins at 200 nM spiked into the human urine; (C) Correct unknown identification accuracy of three sensing systems. There are 24 unknown samples for twelve proteins (Tables S11–S13).

head.ce:title: A colorimetric sensor array for protein discrimination based on carbon nanodots-induced reversible aggregation of AuNP with GSH as a regulator

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, 100048, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, 100048, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology, Beijing Key Lab of Radioactive Waste Treatment, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology
Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology
Beijing Key Lab of Radioactive Waste Treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology, Beijing Key Lab of Radioactive Waste Treatment, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have developed a doubled nanoplasmonic colorimetric sensor array for protein recognition based on carbon nanodots-induced reversible aggregation of gold nanoparticle (AuNP) with GSH as a regulator. Each one well of the array can give two output signals in situ for each target protein by using this strategy, which doubles the sensing unit number of the sensor array from three to six without changing the three-cell configuration, resulting in greatly improved discrimination ability of proteins and identification accuracy of unknown samples. Moreover, our sensing system is very stable in the high concentration of salt, which makes it suitable for real sample analysis.

We have proposed a doubled nanoplasmonic colorimetric sensor array for protein recognition based on car-bon nanodots (CDs)-induced reversible aggregation of gold nanoparticle (AuNP) with GSH as a regulator. It is found that different kinds of CDs can induce the aggregation of AuNP with different degrees and cause different color change. Adding reduced glutathione (GSH) into the CDs/AuNP system can make the AuNP disperse again accompanying with a second color change. Based on this phenomenon, three kinds of CDs originated from different amino acids and citric acid are used to fabricate a colorimetric sensor array with three sensing elements. Upon addition of target proteins into CDs/AuNP conjugates, the competitive protein-AuNP affinity disrupts the SPR absorption peak of CDs/AuNP, generating cross-reactive three-unit colorimetric signal. Subsequently, GSH is introduced into each unit containing protein samples in situ. The SPR absorption peak of AuNP is substantially affected due to the stronger affinity between AuNP and GSH, producing an-other three-unit response signal. With this strategy, each sensing element can give two output signals to each target protein, which doubles the number of unit from three to six without changing the three-cell configuration, resulting in greatly improved discrimination ability of proteins and identification accuracy of unknown samples. Twelve proteins at 50 nM can be well discriminated by this novel colorimetric sensor array. Moreover, our sensing system is very stable in the high concen-tration of salt, make it suitable for the real sample analysis. This method effectively identifies twelve proteins at 200 nM spiked in human urine.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid detection of proteins is essential for biomedical research, clinic diagnosing, and curing of a wide range of diseases [1–3]. Optical sensor arrays (chemical ‘noses/tongues’) have been widely employed for rapid, cost-effective, and high-throughput protein discrimination [4–13]. These systems consist of multiple cross-responsive receptors to generate the specific patterns after interacting with each protein [14–20]. Especially, the colorimetric sensor arrays based on gold nanoparticle (AuNP) have attracted much attention. These assays employ the color-shift of AuNP as their readouts, which is sensitively responsive to the change of interspace among particles, shape, particle sizes, and surrounding medium [21–23]. The AuNP-based colorimetric sensor arrays mainly focus on two sensing strategies including salt-induced aggregation and growth of AuNP (via the reduction of HAuCl4 in the presence of hydroxylamine) for multiple analytes identification [24–28]. However, these sensing systems are unstable and often interfered by many other external factors [29], particularly high concentration of salt in real samples. More importantly, because salt-induced aggregation or growth of AuNP are irreversible processes. So, a single cell of these traditional colorimetric sensor arrays can only provide one read-out signal, which greatly limits the sensing signal number and discrimination power of the sensor array.

To simplify the construction of array and improve the protein discrimination capability, multidimensional sensor arrays (lab-on-a-molecule/nonmaterial) have been configured by integrating multiple unique performance of AuNP, such as high fluorescence quenching, the catalytical properties of artificial mimic enzyme, surface plasmonic resonance behavior. This lab-on-a-nanoparticle approach can offer multidimensional information (fluorescence, colorimetry, and scatting light) for protein discrimination [19,30,31]. However, different optical signals from different sensing mechanisms still require a few diverse detectors. In addition, some colorimetric signals have been obtained from many separated measures or spatial separated wells, resulting in time-consuming analysis and extremely tedious operation process. Another strategy of addressing this issue is to establish sensor elements by using multi-receptors with reversible affinity. For example, Rotello’s group has constructed a fluorescent sensor array coupling the host-guest interaction with receptor-reporter reversible affinity for the discrimination of cancer cell, which effectively doubles the number of output channels in one-well configuration [32]. The increased information benefits to improve the discrimination power of the sensor array. But, strategy has not been realized with colorimetric sensing strategies based AuNP because the irreversible process of salt-induced aggregation and growth of AuNP. Therefore, there are great demands to develop special sensing systems in the fabrication of doubled nanoplasmonic colorimetric sensor arrays based on AuNP with improved discrimination ability for the identification of multiple analytes.

Recently, carbon nanodots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest in bioanalytical work due to their low toxicity, good stability, and fine water solubility [33,34]. CDs have been reported to possess various functional groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic acid, and amino [34], which can be decorated on the AuNP surface through the Au-N and Au-S interaction. The types and number of these functional groups can be tailored by changing the raw materials and synthesis procedure. Thus, CDs surface with differently functional groups are good receptor candidates for sensor arrays. In our previous study, we have found that some CDs can induce the reversible and stable aggregation of AuNP [35,36]. After the addition of GSH, the aggregated CDs/AuNP restores dispersed state with the corresponding color change from blue to red. We assume that this special reversible system with color change can be used to fabricate sensing elements, which offers more signals for sensor array.

In this work, three kinds of CDs synthesized with different carbon sources have shown various affinities toward AuNP, generating differently colorful change due to the reversible aggregation of AuNP (as shown in Scheme 1 ). GSH has stronger affinity towards AuNP and can make AuNP re-dispersed with different degrees, which subsequently produces a second color change signal. By adding a target protein into the CDs/AuNP system, a distinct color pattern with three sensing units can be obtained, reflecting the different affinities among the protein, CDs and AuNP. Furthermore, GSH can adjust the reversible aggregation of AuNP induced by CDs in the presence of various proteins. This change effectively doubles the number of the sensing units from three to six while maintaining three-unit configuration. This increased information content can improve the protein discrimination power. The CDs/AuNP/GSH system is also very stable under high concentration salt, making them suitable for protein discrimination in real samples.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Sodium citrate was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). l-lysine, l-glycine, l-serine, reduced glutathione (GSH) and all the proteins were obtained from Beijing Biodee Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Citric acid was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Shanghai, China). Other regents were also at least analytical pure. Human urine for real sample detection was obtained from healthy male volunteer.

A Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) was used to obtain ultrapure water. 96-well plates (Corning, USA) and SpectraMaxRM2e Multi-Mode Microplate Reader (Molecular Devices, California, USA) were used to record the absorption spectra. A centrifuge (Eppendorf, Germany) was used to centrifuge human urine sample.

AuNP with an average diameter of 13 nm were synthesized according to the procedure described previously [37]. In brief, 20 ml of 40 mM trisodium citrate solution was added into 200 ml 1.0 mM boiling HAuCl4 solution. The solution then kept boiling for 15 min and naturally cooled at room temperature. During the entirely heating procedure, the reaction solution was kept stirring intensely.

Three different kinds of carbon nanodots (CDs) were simply prepared using hydrothermal synthetic route [38]. 10 mM amino acid (lysine, glycine, and serine) and 5 mM citric acid were dissolved in 10 ml of deionized water. Then, the mixture solution was heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The as prepared CDs were labeled as glycine CDs (g-CDs), lysine CDs (l-CDs), and serine CDs (s-CDs).

Twelve different proteins were chosen as analytes, including Trypsin (Try), hemoglobin (Hem), Bovine albumin (BSA), Human Serum Albumin (HSA), Pepsin (Pep), Lysozyme (Lys), Egg Albumin (EA), Myoglobin (Myo), Papain (Pap), Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP), Transferrin (TRF) and Immunoglobulin G (IgG). First, each protein (50 nM) was mixed with AuNP (50 nM) and incubated for 15 min. Then CDs (g-CDs, l-CDs or s-CDs, each 30 μg/mL) were added into the mixed solution. After 5 min incubating, half of the solution was diluted with deionized water and the UV−vis adsorption values at 520 nm and 620 nm were recorded. The other half of the above solution was mixed with GSH (15 μM) and incubated for 20 min, then the solution was diluted with deionized water and the UV–vis adsorption values at 520 nm and 620 nm were measured. Preliminary discrimination of the proteins was conducted in deionized water. The raw data were first normalized (k/k0) to eliminate the potential bias caused by the difference in the original signal intensity of sensing system. The signal value was defined as k = A520 nm/A620 nm, where A520 nm and A620 nm are the adsorption intensity of the solution at 520 nm and at 620 nm, respectively. The ratio value k0 = A520 nm/A620 nm represents adsorption intensity of AuNP. This ratio value (k/k0) can represent the aggregation degree of AuNP.

The urine samples were obtained from healthy male volunteer, and centrifuged for 5 min (12,000 rpm) to remove the insoluble matrix and diluted 20 times for further use. Twelve proteins (final concentration at 200 nM) were spiked in urine solution and detected by the procedure above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversible aggregation of AuNP induced by CDs and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Three kinds of CDs were prepared by using different amino acids (glycine, lysine and serine) and citric acid as raw materials and termed as g-CDs, l-CDs, and s-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, TEM images of the CDs and AuNP illustrate that they dispersed well. The average diameter of AuNP, g-CDs, l-CDs, and s-CDs is about 13.0 nm, 2.3 nm, 2.4 nm, and 2.0 nm, respectively. Our previous work reveals that three types of CDs exhibit typical excitation-dependent behavior. [35] These results demonstrate that three kinds of CDs and AuNP are successfully prepared. FT-IR spectra has been conducted by our coworkers. [38] The results indicated that the CDs generated from different amino acid sources may have various functional groups on their surface (−NH2, −COOH, and −OH). The diverse functional groups of three kinds of CDs have different interaction with the surface of the citrated-coated AuNP through hydrogen bond, van der Waals’ force, and electrostatic interactions, causing the different aggregation/dispersion state of AuNP.

Further, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) behaviors of AuNP induced by three types of CDs were investigated by DLS (Fig. S3) and UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. S4). The results demonstrate that the degree aggregation of AuNP/s-CDs is larger than that of AuNP/l-CDs. AuNP/g-CDs still display the dispersed state of nanoparticles. Following the treatment of s-CDs, the solution color of AuNP/s-CD conjugate turns blue, representing AuNP is severely clustered together into large aggregates as indicated in Fig. 1 (a). Remarkably, after the addition of GSH, the color of the corresponding solution changes from blue to red, suggesting that the aggregated AuNP restored dispersion state due to the stronger affinity between AuNP and GSH (Fig. 1B, inset of photographs). The signal value k (k = A520 nm/A620 nm) can be used to represent the dispersion/aggregation degree of AuNP, and the normalized ratio value (k/k0) is used to eliminate the potential bias caused by the difference in the original signal intensity of sensing system. The smaller value (k/k0) can represent the larger aggregation degree of AuNP. After adding s-CDs to the AuNP, the k/k0 value decreases from 1.0 to 0.53, while this value increases to 0.88 upon the addition of GSH in Fig. 1B, indicating that AuNP changes from aggregation to dispersion. Similarly, l-CDs can also trigger AuNP aggregation as indicated in Fig. 1A (b). The reversible aggregation of AuNP induced by l-CDs still exhibit dispersion state after the addition of GSH due to the stronger affinity between AuNP and GSH. According to the ratio value (k/k0), the size of AuNP aggregation induced by l-CDs is smaller than that with s-CDs in Fig. 1B. On the contrary, the homogeneous dispersion state of AuNP is not disturbed after the addition of g-CDs and the mixture of g-CDs and GSH as illustrated in Fig. 1A (c). The solution color still exhibits wine red after addition of g-CDs in the presence or absence of GSH (Fig. 1B, inset of photographs). The ratio values (k/k0) of these two systems are about 1.0, close to the naked AuNP. These data demonstrate that the aggregation of AuNP induced by CDs is reversible with the addition of GSH, and different types of CDs can give various aggregation/re-disperse response signals.

Further, the CDs-induced aggregation of AuNP is compared with that caused by high concentration salt. It is well- known that in the high ionic strength solution the negatively surface charged density of the AuNP is reduced after the addition of NaCl, resulting in the aggregation of the AuNP accompanying with solution color change from wine red to blue as indicated in Fig. 1C. When GSH is added in the solution, aggregated AuNP cannot be dispersed again. The ratio value (k/k0) in the presence of NaCl is about 0.5, which is almost the same as that of NaCl + GSH as illustrated in Fig. 1D. The solution shows blue color (Fig. 1D, inset of photograph). These data demonstrate that the NaCl-triggered aggregation of AuNP is irreversible, which is much different with the CDs-induced reversible aggregation of AuNP. More importantly, the aggregation of AuNP induced by s-CDs or l-CDs can also be redispersed even in high concentration of salt as displayed in Fig. 1C (d and e). Upon the addition of GSH, the aggregated AuNP can disperse again with the color from blue to red and their ratio values significantly increase in Fig. 1D. The corresponding absorption peaks of AuNP still shift blue from 610 nm/535 nm to 520 nm after the addition of NaCl (Fig. S4B). In the presence of g-CDs, the dispersed AuNP is observed with GSH and NaCl in Fig. 1C (f). Their corresponding ratio values are about 1.0 and solution color remains red as indicated in Fig. 1D. These results demonstrate that GSH can tune the reversible aggregation of AuNP induced by CDs even in the high concentration of salt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of reversible aggregation of AuNP induced by CDs and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of CDs/AuNP conjugate is further investigated, as it is very important for the accuracy and repeatability of the sensor array. The kinetic behaviour of CDs/AuNP aggregation is investigated by monitoring ratio values k/k0 as a function of time. As seen from Fig. 2 A, the ratio value dramatically decreases with 30 min, and then gradually increases in another 3.5 h, revealing unstable aggregation of salt-induced AuNP. Further, the ratio values (k/k0) of AuNP aggregation trigged by s-CDs is the smallest among three CDs/AuNP systems and their solution colour exhibit dark blue in Fig. 2B, indicating the highest degree of aggregation AuNP. It is worth noting that s-CDs/AuNP aggregation remains very stable in the presence of GSH and NaCl. Also, the ratio values (k/k0) of l-CDs/AuNP composite show ignorable changes within 4 h and no precipitate has been observed as illustrated in Fig. 2C. Similarly, the ratio value of AuNP modified by g-CDs does not change with four hours (Fig. 2D) and their corresponding solution colour remain wine red in the presence of GSH and NaCl (Fig. 2D, photographs of inset). In addition, it can rapidly reach equilibrium within a few minutes for the interaction between AuNP and CDs with GSH and NaCl. These results suggest that CDs/AuNP conjugate is very stable through the whole analysis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of a doubled colorimetric sensing elements based on reversible aggregation of CDs/AuNP conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the response behaviors of IgG as a model protein for these three kinds of CDs/AuNP conjugates were studied to prove that whether they can act as doubled sensing elements. The characteristic absorption peak of AuNP/s-CDs or AuNP/l-CDs show clearly red shift along with the color changes in the presence of IgG. It is interesting to note that a red shift of λmax of AuNP/s-CDs is more than that of AuNP/l-CDs. The color of the corresponding solution exhibits blue (Fig. 3 A, photograph 2 and 3). According to the determination of DLS (Fig. 4 B), the average size of AuNP/s-CDs is about 190 nm and the aggregated AuNP is also seen in TEM images. AuNP/s-CDs exhibits more severely aggregation than that of AuNP/l-CDs by virtue of the particle size distribution of DLS and TEM images (Fig. 4C). As illustrated in Fig. 3A, absorption spectra and solution color for AuNP/g-CDs without IgG is almost the same as that with IgG protein, which are similarly absorption profile of naked AuNP. Their solution color shows wine red (Fig. 3A, photograph 1 and 4). As can be seen from TEM images and DLS results, the average size of nanoparticles of these systems do not show obvious changes and monodispersed AuNP are obtained (Fig. 4A, D and E). When GSH was added into the solution, the characteristic peaks of AuNP/s-CDs, AuNP/l-CDs, and AuNP/g-CDs are blue-shifted and their maximum absorbance appears at 520 nm again in Fig. 3B. The color of the corresponding solution changes from blue to red (Fig. 3B, photograph 5, 6, and 7). According to the detection of DLS and TEM images, s-CDs, l-CDs, g-CDs, and IgG protein can desorb from AuNP surface due to the stronger affinity between AuNP and GSH (Fig. 4F–H). At the same time, the ratio values (k/k0) for AuNP/s-CDs and AuNP/l-CDs respectively increase by approximate 116.3% and 60.4% in Fig. 3C, suggesting that AuNP aggregation is well dispersed again.

Further, the response behaviors of single CDs with different proteins for CDs/AuNP conjugates were also explored. Three different proteins with various pI (Pap, IgG, BSA, pI = 8.8, 7.5, 4.8, respectively.) were chosen as models interacting with AuNP/s-CDs (Fig. S6). As illustrated in Fig. S6A, λmax of AuNP/s-CDs of both the system with Pap and IgG show clear red shift in absorption, and the solution color also changes from wine red to purple, while the system with BSA shows no obvious change. After adding GSH, blue shift of λmax of AuNP/s-CDs is observed in the system with Pap and IgG, as shown in Fig. S6B. Notably, the blue shift of AuNP/s-CDs with IgG after adding GSH is more than that of AuNP/s-CDs with Pap. These results indicate that different proteins with diverse structural characteristics cause different color changing even in the same AuNP/CDs conjugates system.

Based on these results, six cross-reactive response signals of IgG protein are generated from three sensing units of CDs/AuNP conjugates as shown schematically in Fig. 3D. Therefore, these data demonstrate that doubled sensing strategy is feasible for proteins discrimination based on the reversible aggregation of CDs/AuNP modulated by GSH.

In order to obtain a better discrimination capability, we investigated the effect of pH toward the aggregation process of AuNP/CDs, the optimum concentration of CDs and GSH. As illustrated in Fig. S7, the aggregation power of s-CDs is weakened in alkaline solution. On the contrary, the reversible aggregation of AuNP was not affected in acidic and neutral environment, which indicate that the aggregation process of CDs is more suitable in neutral and acidic environment. Thus, our experiment was employed at neural solution, which is consistent with the solution pH 7.4 in biological sample.

As shown in Fig. S8A, AuNP (5 nM) with different concentration of s-CDs (0–50 μg/mL) aggregated within 5 min reaction time, the ratio value k/k0 reached the platform from 15 μg/mL, the final concentration of s-CDs was chosen at 30 μg/mL. the optimized GSH concentration is shown in Fig. S8B AuNP (5 nM) and s-CDs (30 μg/mL) with different concentration of GSH (0–30 μM), the ratio value k/k0 reached the platform from 10 μM, the final concentration of GSH was chosen at 15 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A doubled colorimetric sensor array for discrimination of multiple proteins
ce:section.ce:para: This doubled colorimetric sensor array composed of three sensing units (s-CDs/AuNP, l-CDs/AuNP, and g-CDs/AuNP) are further used for various protein discrimination. Twelve proteins (each protein at final concentration at 50 nM) with different molecule weight, isoelectric point, and chemical structure were selected as sensing targets (Table S1). Upon the addition of target proteins, the competitive protein/AuNP affinity disrupts the SPR absorption peak of CDs/AuNP, generating the distinct fingerprint and inducing the change of the solution color. Because proteins can interact with AuNP through various forces, including electrostatic, van der Waals’ force, hydrogen bonding, steric, and hydrophobic feature. [39,40] Next, GSH was introduced in this array containing twelve proteins in situ and the SPR peaks of CDs/AuNP with different proteins also appear some changes owing to the stronger affinity between GSH and AuNP. The subsequent readout signals were also recorded. As a result, the number of readout signals for twelve proteins against this array is doubled from three to six units. The fingerprint response pattern of UV–vis absorbance for each protein is illustrated in Fig. 5 A. Each protein can be reproducibly discerned by the UV–vis absorption response patterns. The pattern differences among analytes can be easily observed even by the naked eye in Fig. 5B. Besides, to show the difference between each unit more completely, the color difference map of our proposed method is presented in Supporting Information. As can be seen in Fig. S9A, the color map is obtained by calculating the difference of the RGB value of the optical image (Fig. S9A) and the blank group (Fig. S9B).

To visualize the fingerprints more clearly, LDA is employed to quantitatively differentiate the doubled colorimetric response patterns of the conjugates of CDs/AuNP with the various proteins. Six replicates are tested for each protein. These ratios of response colorimetric signal are subjected to linear discriminant analysis (LDA). Cluster separation on an LDA plot is given based on their standardized Mahalanobis distance.

The probability of misclassification can be lower with increasing the distance. The two discrimination factors are employed to generate a two-dimensional (2D) plot as shown in Fig. 6 A–C, in which each point represents the response pattern for an individual protein sample against the sensor array. When only three sensing elements (g-CDs/AuNP, l-CDs/AuNP, and s-CDs/AuNP) without addition of GSH are used, the distinct response patterns (3 sensing elements × 12 proteins × 6 replicates) are clustered into 12 distinct groups in Fig. 6A. The majority of the protein targets (8 out of 12) are clearly identified. However, four proteins (Hem/Pap, and Try/IgG) exhibit significant overlap between their 95% confidence ellipses (marked in the inlet), suggesting the limited discrimination ability of three CDs/AuNP transducer. The results obtained from the jackknifed classification matrix shows that the classification accuracy is 86%. While only another three sensing elements upon the addition of GSH are employed to identify proteins, the response patterns of six groups substantially overlap between HSA with Pep, BSA with Pep, and Try with Myo as shown in Fig. 6B. The classification accuracy of twelve proteins is 86%. Furthermore, when doubled three sensing units are combined, the corresponding three-dimensional LDA plot is illustrated in Fig. 6C.

And then multiple canonical colorimetric response patterns (3 sensing elements×12 proteins × 6 replicates × 2 doubled signal) are clustered into 12 distinct groups, which is built to gain three canonical factors (53.5%, 28.2%, and 11.9%). Remarkably, all twelve proteins are well separated and the classification accuracy is up to 100%.

The critical challenge for the protein identification against a new sensor array is to achieve the good reproducibility. Some unknown samples were randomly taken from the training set. The new cases were classified to the groups generated through the training matrix according to their Mahalanobis distances by using LDA. Out of 24 samples, 21 are identified correctly only by using three units of CDs/AuNP without GSH, which indicates 87.5% with the identification accuracy. While the discrimination accuracy of these same unknown samples is 79.2% only from another three CDs/AuNP with GSH. Importantly, all unknown samples are discriminated by combining three sensing elements without GSH and with GSH, whose identification accuracy is found to be 100% at the 50 nM level (Fig. 6D and Tables S5–S7). These results demonstrate that the novel strategy of doubled sensor array is significant for improving the ability of protein discrimination.

In order to test the ability of this colorimetric sensor array in biological environment, the discrimination procedure was carried out in human urine samples. As a kind of complex biological sample, there are overall protein content at more than 1.5 μM, more multianalyte nature (more than 1500 proteins as competing biomolecules), and high concentration of salt, creating a complicated environment for protein discrimination. For our study, twelve proteins (final concentration at 200 nM for each protein) were spiked into urine sample, and the other experimental condition was remained the same with the above colorimetric sensor array. Finally, the urine samples spiked by multiple proteins are diluted 20 times and the concentration of salt is about 8.0 mM. As was shown in Fig. 7 A, twelve proteins can be well separated by using doubled three sensing units with six-unit signal and their distinct fingerprint has been offered (Fig. S10A and Tables S8–S10). However, when only three sensing units without GSH are used to identify proteins spiked into the urine, there are always some overlaps between Try and Pep (Fig. S10A). While only three sensing units with GSH are employed to discriminate these same protein samples, there are substantial overlap among these proteins especially EA, IgG and Lys, HRP and Hem (Fig. S10B). This colorimetric response pattern of proteins against six readout signals are processed by using hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), which provided a better alternative for accurate representation and classification of high dimensional data based on Euclidean distance. From the dendrogram generated by HCA in Fig. 7B, all of these twelve proteins are accurately differentiated from one another with no error or misclassification in six replicates experiments. Similar trends are observed in the % CUI of these proteins spiked into the urine samples, where the six units give much better unknown identification of 95.8% (23 out of 24 samples) compared to the three units (Fig. 7C and Tables S11–S13). The detection limit of this doubled colorimetric sensor array is down to 100 nM for twelve proteins discrimination spiked in the urine with accuracy of 99% (Fig. S11). These results of twelve proteins spiked in urine confirm the benefit of increasing the number of readout signals in a chemical nose sensing system. The classification accuracy of training group does not only increase, but also the identification accuracy in unknown samples also greatly increases.

To prove that our sensor array still has the accurate discrimination power in complex biological sample. The interference of some ions such as Ca2+ and Mg2+ which are present in the urine sample was also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S12, Ca2+ and Mg2+ were both spiked into water sample and human urine sample to test the discrimination power of this sensor array toward several proteins. The results confirmed that these proteins still can be differentiate in the presence of interfere ions with 100% accuracy.

In summary, we have developed doubled nanoplasmonic colorimetric sensor array based on GSH-modulated reversible aggregation of CDs/AuNP for protein discrimination. A new system utilizing reversible aggregation has been developed for the discrimination of proteins, based on three CDs originated from different amino acids and citric acid interacting with AuNP, acting as three sensing units. This method effectively identifies twelve proteins at 50 nM, as well as twelve proteins at 200 nM spiked in human urine. Compared to Previous works based on the salt-induced aggregation and growth of AuNP, this sensing system has some advantages as following. Firstly, this sensor array is stable with high concentration of slat and more suitable for the complicated biological samples. Secondly, the multiple signals can be achieved in one well in situ because CDs-induced aggregation of AuNP is a reversible process. The discrimination power of this array is greatly improved for the detection of proteins. Finally, this strategy requires less quantity of samples and more simple operation compared to the traditional multidimensional sensor array. In a broader context, this CDs-induced reversible aggregation of AuNP with GSH as a regulator presents a general strategy of increasing dimensionality in sensor arrays.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21105066, No. 21775087), Beijing Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 2162010), and Scientific Research Project of Beijing Educational Committee (Grant No. KM201610028008), and Cross-Disciplinary Research Project of Capital Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.126677.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
